coredata.dc:title: Reliable strategy of sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for sensing silver nanoparticles using specific carbon dots derived from mercaptoacetic acid and melamine
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hongyuan
Liu, Yongli
Wu, Yalin
Zhou, Qingxiang
Li, Zhi
Jiang, Liushan
Chen, Chunmao
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur element co-decorated carbon nanodots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized by solid state hydrothermal method utilizing mercaptoacetic acid and melamine as the precursors. The obtained N,S-CDs had wonderful optical and chemical stability. The experimental results demonstrated that silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) could noticeably quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. The quenching of fluorescence signal from the presence of AgNPs may be attributed to inner filter effect. The crafted nanoprobe for sensing AgNPs was endowed with some specialties such as simplicity, excellent selectivity and sensitivity, environmental friendliness and low cost. The probe exhibited specific linearity from 0.024 to 1.77 nM, and was endowed a good limit of detection down to 0.022 nM. The experimental results demonstrated that the built probe could be an efficient tool for AgNPs detection and had a prospective application, and also provided a new direction for establishing innovative method for determining and monitoring pollutants from nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated carbon nanodots
Silver nanoparticles
Fluorescent nanoprobe

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: CEST2021 highlights: Recent findings on occurrence, analytical methods, treatment and environmental effects of pollutants, edited by Vincenzo Naddeo, Shadi hasan, and Raul Muñoz

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the analytical parameters for sensing AgNPs with N,S-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21976211 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N,S-CDs and its examining mechanism of AgNPs.
The characterization of N,S-CDs. (A) TEM image. (B) UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. (C) Fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs at various excitation wavelength. (D) The FT-IR spectra.
Fluorescence decay curves of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs + AgNPs.

Optimization of experimental parameters. (A) Fluorescence quenching intensity change in different proportion of the TGA. (B) Effect of concentration of N,S-CDs on sensing AgNPs. (C) Fluorescence intensity and (D) fluorescence quenching intensity change of N,S-CDs under various pH. (E) Effect of incubation time on ΔF value of N,S-CDs by AgNPs. (F) Effect of ion strength on the fluorescent signal of N,S-CDs.

(A) Fluorescent responses of N,S-CDs on different concentration of AgNPs (from top to bottom, 0.024, 0.059, 0.118, 0.236, 0.413, 0.59, 0.885, 1.18, 1.475, 1.77 nM). (B) The calibrate curve of N,S-CDs to various concentration of AgNPs.
Selectivity of the fluorescence nanoprobe for sensing AgNPs.
Spiked recoveries of AgNPs in real river waters.

head.ce:title: Reliable strategy of sensitive fluorescent nanoprobe for sensing silver nanoparticles using specific carbon dots derived from mercaptoacetic acid and melamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, 453007, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Environmental Protection, Beijing 100037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Environmental Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Environmental Protection, Beijing 100037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur element co-decorated carbon nanodots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized by solid state hydrothermal method utilizing mercaptoacetic acid and melamine as the precursors. The obtained N,S-CDs had wonderful optical and chemical stability. The experimental results demonstrated that silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) could noticeably quench the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. The quenching of fluorescence signal from the presence of AgNPs may be attributed to inner filter effect. The crafted nanoprobe for sensing AgNPs was endowed with some specialties such as simplicity, excellent selectivity and sensitivity, environmental friendliness and low cost. The probe exhibited specific linearity from 0.024 to 1.77 nM, and was endowed a good limit of detection down to 0.022 nM. The experimental results demonstrated that the built probe could be an efficient tool for AgNPs detection and had a prospective application, and also provided a new direction for establishing innovative method for determining and monitoring pollutants from nanoparticles.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated carbon nanodots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are metal-based nanoparticles and have drawn worldwide attention from various scientists in different scientific fields due to their good physicochemical and biological properties. They are widely used in chemical analysis, pharmaceutical manufacturing, antimicrobial materials, consumer products, electrocatalysts, and photocatalysts (Cheng et al., 2018; Han et al., 2018; Peiris et al., 2018; Sana and Dogiparthi, 2018; Shuai et al., 2018; Sukhorukova et al., 2018; Barrientos et al., 2018), etc. Especially, various commercial products contained AgNPs including textiles, plastic products, electronics and washing machines have been extensively used, and AgNPs can be released into the environment during their lifecycle (Chawla et al., 2017). As a result, natural eco-environment is continually exposed to anthropogenic pollutants which causes damage to human health and the environment. AgNPs have been reported to be toxicity on organisms, and the main exposure pathways for human will be skin, gastrointestinal and respiratory systems (Chen and Schluesener, 2008), etc. Arora et al. (Arora et al., 2008, 2009) has found that low doses of AgNPs produced apoptosis and higher doses produced necrosis to human fibrosarcoma and human skin. Human body intakes AgNPs by respiratory tract attributed to inhalation of haze or fume which contains AgNPs. What's more, they may be ingested from water, food vessels disposed with silver nanoparticles, or by penetrating skin during the utilization of cosmetics and textiles added with AgNPs. AgNPs can accumulate and damage lungs and liver and so on, or penetrate the blood-brain barrier after entering into the rodents' body (Arora et al., 2009; Braydich-Stolle et al., 2005; Hussain et al., 2005). It has already proved that AgNPs can be genotoxic in human cells (AshaRani et al., 2009) and toxic to bacteria obtained from sewage disposal systems (Choi and Hu, 2008), and cause serious damage to environmental health (Shevlin et al., 2018). There is evidence that AgNPs exists in wastewater and natural waters (Hoque et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2018). It is extremely imperative to develop new and convenient analytical methods for AgNPs.

As AgNPs are concerned, many methods have been developed. Single-particle inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry is an effective analytical tool (Mitrano et al., 2012). Total reflection X-ray fluorescence analysis can be used for analyzing AgNPs in combination with cloud point extraction, which resulted in low detection limit of 0.3 μg L−1(Bahadir et al., 2018). Magnetic chitosan microsphere enrichment before analysis with inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry can also increase the sensitivity of AgNPs (Tolessa et al., 2017). Inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry is also a good alternative for AgNPs detection, combination with aminopropylsilica solid phase extraction can enhance the detection performance with detection limit of 34.01 mg g−1(Anekthirakun and Imyim, 2019). Recently, atomic absorption spectrometry coupled with dispersive suspended microextraction was reported to quantify AgNPs in environmental samples with good selectivity and sensitivity (Choleva et al., 2019). These methods are very effective for the detection of AgNPs, however, most of them need expensive instruments and an oxidative procedure to transfer AgNPs into Ag ions. Except these methods, fluorescence method is considered to be one of the most promising analytical methods. Fluorescence sensors usually have high sensitivity, portability and short response time. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are the first reported fluorescent nanosized carbon particles (Xu et al., 2004). What's more, CDs can be modified with various functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and carbonyl groups through other compounds or depending on the raw materials. Usually, they have the excellent properties including stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and high aqueous solubility. Hence, CDs have been widely used in catalysis for degradation of contaminants and hydrogen evolution, sensors for measuring cations, anions and organic pollutants, imaging, and optoelectronic devices (Zhong et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2021, 2022; Lu et al., 2017; Sun and Lei, 2017; Pongprom et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022; Cao et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2021; Sheng et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020).

In present study, melamine and mercaptoacetic acid were utilized as nitrogen and sulfur precursors to synthesize crafted nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated carbon nanodots (N,S-CDs), which was employed to construct a reliable fluorescence nanoprobe for selectively sensing AgNPs (Fig. 1 ). Melamine is a good nitrogen precursor with high levels of nitrogen. The characterization of obtained N,S-CDs with related instruments was explored. The important parameters were also examined.

Chromium Chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O, 99%), melamine (99%), cadmium nitrate (CdNO3·4H2O, 99%), polyvinylpyrrolidone were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Copper sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O) were gained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. NaCl, cobalt nitrate and zinc nitrate were provided by Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. Ltd. Calcium chloride (CaCl2·5H2O), manganese chloride (MnCl2·4H2O), potassium iodide, silver nitrate, mercuric chloride, hydrochloride, sodium hydroxide were bought from Beijing Chemical Factory. Thioacetic acid was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Magnesium chloride was obtained from Beijing Tongguang Fine Chemical Company. All of the reagents are analytical grade if no special instructions.

Fluorescence lifetimes were performed on an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, Edinburgh, UK). The other instruments used for determination and characterization in this work were provided in supplementary material.

Following previous reported method (Iqbal et al., 2018), photoluminescent N,S-CDs were fabricated by solid state hydrothermal method with mercaptoacetic acid and melamine at different ratios. They were added with different ratios into a Teflon-lined without any additives and reacted for 4 h with temperature controlled at 200 °C. The reaction products are cooled naturally in cool and ventilated place. The produced yellow solid particles were dissolved with 20 mL water, then filtered through a microporous membrane with a diameter of 0.2 μm. The obtained filtrate was dialyzed for 3 h using a 200 molecular weight dialysis bag and then dried. The obtained N,S-CDs was dissolved with water to achieve stock solution with a concentration of 5 mg mL−1, and conserved at 4 °C.

4 mL of silver nitrate solution (0.01 mol L−1) and 40 mL of deionized water were mixed, and in which 4 mL 0.01 mol L−1 polyvinylpyrrolidone solution was added dropwise under stirring in ice water bath in 20 min in which polyvinylpyrrolidone was used as the stable reagent avoiding the agglomeration of the synthetized AgNPs. After that, 2 mL 3.8 mg mL−1 newly prepared sodium borohydride solution were added drop by drop and the color of solution turned yellow rapidly, and constant agitated for 5 min under the ice water bath and under normal temperature for 15 min. Further brownish yellow solution containing AgNPs was obtained, and stored in brown bottle and in dark before further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the analytical parameters for sensing AgNPs with N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this procedure, a series of concentrations of CDs, incubation time and pH value of reaction medium were tested to obtain optimal conditions of N,S-CDs for sensing. In the sensing procedure, the excitation and emission wavelengths were fixed at 325 nm and 409 nm and the slit widths were kept at 5 nm, respectively. A series of metal cations were used to assess the disturbing effect of coexisting ions on the detection of AgNPs.

50 μL N,S-CDs and specific volume of AgNPs solution with various levels were diluted to 1 mL (The eventual concentration of N,S-CDs, 250 μg mL−1). The solution was mixed thoroughly and placed at ambient temperature for several minutes, then its fluorescence spectrum was recorded.

Fig. S1A exhibits the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of AgNPs. It was found that the shape of AgNPs was spherical. They are fairly evenly distributed, and the mean size was about 9.48 nm, which was statistically calculated from 120 random AgNPs. The solution of AgNPs had a strong absorption peak around 395 nm (Fig. S1B). The molar extinction coefficient (ε) of AgNPs was about 5.77 × 108 M−1 cm−1, which was calculated by the formula based on Mie theory (Navarro and Werts, 2013), and the concentration of AgNPs was 1.18 × 10−8 M calculated based on Lambert-Beer law (the related principles were provided in detail in supplementary).

Fig. 2 A exhibits the TEM image of N,S-CDs. It was evident that the shape of N,S-CDs was also nearly spherical and the average size was about 10.15 nm. As exhibited from Fig. 2B, the red line was the emission spectrum of N,S-CDs with excitation at 325 nm, the black line was UV absorbance spectrum of N,S-CDs, and blue line was UV absorbance spectrum of AgNPs, independently. It was obvious that there was an intersection between the UV absorption spectrum of AgNPs and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the obtained N,S-CDs. Moreover, the absorption and emission spectra of N,S-CDs had no intersection, so they did not absorb the fluorescence emitted by themselves. Fig. 2C illuminates the fluorescence emission spectra of the obtained N,S-CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 285 nm to 355 nm. The FL emission wavelengths of N,S-CDs occurred a blue shift with excitation wavelength at lower than 325 nm and then a red shift appeared with excitation wavelength at higher than 325 nm. The strongest fluorescence emission intensity was achieved with excitation wavelength at 325 nm. Fig. 2D shows the spectrum of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of N,S-CD. As seen from Fig. 2D, the band centered at 3200 cm−1 was due to the stretching-vibration of –NH2 group and absorption band at 1575 cm−1 was attributed to the bending-vibration of N–H group. A broad absorption band at 2520 cm−1 might be the stretching-vibration of –SH group. The bands centered at 1665 cm−1 and 1190 cm−1 were related with –CO, -C-O stretching-vibration. The fluorescence quantum yield of target N,S-CDs was examined with a reported method (Liu et al., 2015, 2017), and the obtained relative fluorescence quantum yield was 2.1% (the calculation principle was provided in supplementary materials).

As fluorescence quenching is concerned, the two mechanisms of inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) are often involved. Generally, the same requirement of these two mechanisms is the presence of spectral overlap. IFE occurs under the absorption of excitation and/or emission light by the analytes in the detection system. However FRET occurs with the emission spectrum of the CDs overlapping with the absorption spectrum of the analyte in the detection system. As exhibited from Fig. 2B, the fluorescence emission spectrum of N,S-CDs crossed the absorption spectrum of AgNPs, indicating that the fluorescence quenching might be related to inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). To further explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,S-CDs, the fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CDs without and with the presence of different concentration of AgNPs were measured with an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Fig. 3 ). It was found that the fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CDs and the mixture of N,S-CDs and AgNPs were 7.3306 ns and 6.8958 ns, independently. The results showed that there was a non-remarkably reduction in the average lifespan of the target N,S-CDs with presence of AgNPs, and this result was very same as the reported by Yang et al. (2021), and which was very near to IFE because the quencher in the IFE process does not alter the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescent reagent (Zu et al., 2017). Therefore, the observed fluorescence quenching should be caused by IFE.

The ratio of the two precursors will have crucial effect, and it was explored in order to obtain the ideal N,S-CDs. During this procedure, the mass of melamine was fixed (0.3 g) and the ratio of them was changed by changing the volume of thioglycolic acid (TGA) (0, 0.15, 0.3, 0.45, 0.6, 0.75 and 0.9 mL). The fluorescence quenching effect of the correspondingly obtained N,S-CDs was demonstrated in Fig. 4 A. It was apparent that the change of fluorescent emission intensity (ΔF) rose following the increase of TGA volume within the range of 0–0.3 mL, and slowly dropped with the continuous increase of TGA volume. The results demonstrated that maximum ΔF was achieved with 0.3 mL TGA.

The concentration of N,S-CDs is a key parameter for the nanoprobe because it will provide enough fluorescence intensity for developing the nanoprobe, but it cannot be too high due to the fact that too high concentration will result in strong fluorescence emission intensity, which will not clearly exhibit the tiny fluorescence emission intensity change from the quenching by lower concentration of AgNPs, and self-priming phenomenon will appear and the analytical results will be affected. Moreover, a wide linear change also needs a higher initial concentration of N,S-CDs. Hence a suitable concentration should be investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the ΔF increased rapidly with the increase of N,S-CDs concentration from 25 μg mL−1 to 250 μg mL−1, and remained constant thereafter with no significant change over 250 μg mL−1. Therefore, 250 μg mL−1 was chosen as the best concentration.

Sample pH is a significant factor, and it will affect the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs and ΔF with presence of AgNPs. Fig. 4C shows the fluorescence properties of N,S-CDs at various sample pH. It was clear that strong acid and strong alkali environment would significantly affect the fluorescent properties of N,S-CDs because of the protonation process and deprotonation process. N,S-CDs resulted in stable fluorescence with imperceptible fluorescence change in pH range of pH 4–11. This result was very similar to that of previous report (Chen et al., 2017). That is to say, in this pH range, the fluorescence quenching may be ascribe to other aspects not by pH. Fig. 4D exhibits the fluorescence intensity quenched by AgNPs at different pH value. Apparently, ΔF increased noticeably when the solution pH changed in the range of pH2-7, and then decreased rapidly over pH7-14. In pH 4–11, the quenched fluorescence was attributed to the addition of AgNPs. The best quenching performance was gained when pH value was adjusted to pH 7, which meant that pH7 was optimal for sensing AgNPs.

The incubation time is a necessary parameter in the development of nanoprobe. Reasonable incubation time can greatly shorten the analytical time and result in best detection performance. The optimization results of the incubation time was shown in Fig. 4E, it was obvious that the two substrates reacted and fluorescence quenching occurred very quickly, and the quenched fluorescence intensity change value increased rapidly to the maximum value and kept constant in the time range of 1–120 min. It took only 2 min or even shorter incubation time to reach the end of the reaction after AgNPs was added. So only a little time was spent to sense AgNPs in the solution.

Ionic strength is another crucial parameter that has vital impact on fluorescence stability and fluorescence intensity. Here it was explored in range of 0–5 molL-1 with addition of NaCl to adjust the solution ionic strength. From Fig. 4F, it was found that addition of NaCl had little effect on the fluorescent emission intensity of the obtained N,S-CDs as NaCl concentration was less than 0.1 molL-1. As NaCl concentration continuously raised, the fluorescent emission intensity of N,S-CDs declined slightly, and kept almost constant when the NaCl concentration was in the range of 0.5–5 molL-1. The ionic strength in the actual water is generally small, it can be inferred that this nanoprobe could be employed to measure the concentration of AgNPs in the actual water sample without being greatly disturbed.

As a new nanoprobe was constructed, the feature parameters including the linearity and limit of detection of AgNPs should be examined. Related designed experiments were carried out, and the results were presented in Fig. 5 . The fluorescent emission intensity declined along with the increase of the concentration of AgNPs in the range of 0.024–1.77 nM (See Fig. 5A). The quenched fluorescence emission intensity change value at 409 nm was proportional to the concentration of AgNPs (see Fig. 5B). An excellent linearity was found in the range of 0.024–1.77 nM (R = 0.9957). In addition, the linear regression equation was obtained as ΔF = 1397.30 c+59.44. The detection limit of AgNPs was 0.022 nM based on 3σ/s (σ is the standard deviation of blank signal, and s is the slope of the calibration curve, and n = 20). Up to date, there are very few literatures based on the fluorescence probe for the determination of AgNPs. Chatterjee et al. reported a Rhodamine B derivative fluorescence probe for sensing AgNPs in the range of 1–10 μM, in which AgNPs were undergone an oxidation reaction by H2O2 under acidic environment (Chatterjee et al., 2009). Petdum et al. prepared an effective “turn-on” mode Ag+ fluorescence probe based on 2-(4-(2-aminoethylsulfanyl) butylsulfanyl)ethanamine and [5]helicene dye, and further AgNPs were detected by oxidizing and transferring into Ag+ in the range of 0–835 μM (Petdum et al., 2018). Cayuela et al. modified carbon dots derived from multiwalled carbon nanotubes with ethylenediamine and developed a selective fluorescence probe for AgNPs based on inner filter effect (IFE), which owned detection limit of 5.17 μM (Cayuela et al., 2015). Hence, in contrast this developed fluorescent nanoprobe were simple, economical and convenient, which indicated it may be a useful and robust tool for sensing AgNPs.

The selectivity of this developed nanoprobe for sensing AgNPs was evaluated with metal ions spiked at 2 μM under the optimal conditions. Designed experiments were performed to validate the selectivity of the nanoprobe for measuring AgNPs against other metal ions, including Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, K+, As3+, As5+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Ag+. The results were shown in Fig. 6 . Firstly, they were investigated individually and the quenched fluorescence intensities were exhibited as purple bars in Fig. 6. It was obvious that high concentration of metal ions resulted in ignored effect on the ΔF values except for AgNPs. The distinguishing results could be accounted to the fact that AgNPs had a very powerful affinity with the carboxylate, organic nitrogen or sulfhydryl groups of N,S-CDs than other metal ions. Furthermore, AgNPs and each metal ion mentioned above was blended with N,S-CDs solution, and the corresponding ΔF (red bars) was measured. It was clear that concomitant metal ion showed no significant influence, indicating that the target N,S-CDs had much higher selectivity for AgNPs.

The utility of the nanoprobe was also paid more attention, three water samples were collected from Olympic Sports Center, Nansha River, Yuyuantan Park for the investigation. The samples were pretreated using microporous membrane with a diameter of 0.22 μm. After that, these samples were analyzed directly according to the optimal parameters, and then spiked ones with AgNPs at three concentrations of 0.118, 0.58 and 1.18 nM were analyzed simultaneously. The results were exhibited in Table 1 . The data indicated that no AgNPs were found in these water samples, and for validating the nanoprobe the spiked water samples were also examined. The spiked recoveries were very good from 96.5% to 105.2%. All these data demonstrated that the constructed nanoprobe was a rapid and valuable tool for sensing trace AgNPs in aqueous environment.

In present study, a new one-step solid state hydrothermal method was described to prepare N,S-CDs from melamine and mercaptoacetic acid. As-obtained N,S-QDs possessed excitation-dependent emission property. The as-obtained CDs exhibited specificity to sensing AgNPs and could be utilized as a sensitive and valuable nanoprobe for determination of AgNPs. The quenching mechanism was contributed to IFE. The experimental results demonstrated that the nanoprobe was endowed with reliability, high sensitivity and selectivity, and environmental friendliness. This work provided a new direction to explore new methods for pollutants from nanoparticles, and the solid state hydrothermal method would have application prospect.

Hongyuan Wang: Project administration, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yongli Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yalin Wu: Investigation, Software. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization. Zhi Li: Investigation, Software, Validation. Liusan Jiang: Investigation. Chunmao Chen: Investigation, Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2022.136268.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Separation of silver ions and silver nanoparticles by silica based-solid phase extraction prior to ICP-OES determination
Microchem. J.

Toxicol. Lett.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.
